subject: Chemistry
q: Complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an oxygen containing compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>64</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>08</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The percentage of oxygen in the organic compound is :
question: Complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an oxygen containing compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>64</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>08</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The percentage of oxygen in the organic compound is :
solution:
nCO2=2.6444=0.06

nc=0.06

weight of carbon =0.06×12=0.72 gm

nH2O=1.0818=0.06

nH=0.06×2=0.12

 weight of H=0.12 gm

= 1.8 - (0.72 + 0.12)

= 0.96 gram

 % weight of oxygen =0.961.8×100

The percentage of oxygen in the organic compound = 53.3%.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The elemental composition of a compound is $54.2 \% \mathrm{C}, 9.2 \% \mathrm{H}$ and $36.6 \% \mathrm{O}$.
If the molar mass of the compound is $132 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the molecular formula of the compound is :
[Given : The relative atomic mass of $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{H}: \mathrm{O}=12: 1: 16$ ]
question:
The elemental composition of a compound is $54.2 \% \mathrm{C}, 9.2 \% \mathrm{H}$ and $36.6 \% \mathrm{O}$.
If the molar mass of the compound is $132 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the molecular formula of the compound is :
[Given : The relative atomic mass of $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{H}: \mathrm{O}=12: 1: 16$ ]
solution: Molecular mass of compound $=132 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ Molecular formula of compound is $\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4 \mathrm{O}\right)_n$ $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\mathrm{EF} \text { mass }}=\frac{132}{44}=3$ $\therefore$ Molecular formula of compound is $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_3$

options: <math><mn>24</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon and <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
<math><mn>18</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon annd <math><mn>7</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
<math><mn>22</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon and <math><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
<math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> carbon and <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
solution:
2 mole of CO2 contains 2 mole of carbon, Hence weight of carbon =2×12=24 g .
mass of Hydrogen =25-24=1 gram

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List-I with List-II
  List-I   List-II
A 16 g of CH4 (g) I Weighs 28g
B 1 g of H2(g) II 60.2 × 1023 electrons
C 1 mole of N2 (g) III Weighs 32g
D 0.5 mol of SO2(g) IV Occupies11.4 L volume at STP

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List-I with List-II
  List-I   List-II
A 16 g of CH4 (g) I Weighs 28g
B 1 g of H2(g) II 60.2 × 1023 electrons
C 1 mole of N2 (g) III Weighs 32g
D 0.5 mol of SO2(g) IV Occupies11.4 L volume at STP

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The number of moles =MassMolar mass

The number of molecules = moles×NA

One mole of any gas occupies 22.7 L at STP.

(A) Moles of CH4=1616=1
Molecules =6.02×1023
Electrons =10×6.02×1023
=60.2×1023

(B) Moles of H2=12=0.5 mole
Volume at STP =12×22.7=11.35 lit .

(C) 1 mole N2=28gm

(D) 0.5 mole SO2=12×64=32gm

subject: Chemistry
q: 0.05 cm thick coating of silver is deposited on a plate of 0.05 m2 area. The number of silver atoms deposited on plate are _______×1023. (At mass Ag =108, d=7.9 g cm-3 Round off to the nearest integer.
question: 0.05 cm thick coating of silver is deposited on a plate of 0.05 m2 area. The number of silver atoms deposited on plate are _______×1023. (At mass Ag =108, d=7.9 g cm-3 Round off to the nearest integer.
solution:
Volume of silver coating =0.05×0.05×10000=25 cm3

Given, Density =7.9 g cm-3

Density = Mass/ Volume 

Mass of silver deposited =25×7.9 g

Moles of silver atoms = mass / molar mass =25×7.9108

Number of silver atoms =25×7.9108×6.023×1023

=11.01×1023

Ans. 11

subject: Chemistry
q:
The density of $3 \mathrm{M}$ solution of sodium chloride is $1.252 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$. The molality of the solution will be :
$\left(\right.$ molar mass, $\left.\mathrm{NaCl}=585 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
question:
The density of $3 \mathrm{M}$ solution of sodium chloride is $1.252 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$. The molality of the solution will be :
$\left(\right.$ molar mass, $\left.\mathrm{NaCl}=585 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
solution:
The relation between molarity (M) and molality $(m)$ is
$d=\mathrm{M}\left(\frac{1}{m}+\frac{\mathrm{M}_2}{1000}\right), \mathrm{M}_2=$ Mol. mass of solute
On putting value
$$
1.252=3\left(\frac{1}{m}+\frac{58.5}{1000}\right)
$$
on solving $m=2.79$

subject: Chemistry
q: The ratio of number of oxygen atoms $(\mathrm{O})$ in $16.0 \mathrm{~g}$ ozone $\left(\mathrm{O}_3\right), 28.0 \mathrm{~g}$ carbon monoxide $(\mathrm{CO})$ and $16.0$ oxygen $\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)$ is (Atomic mass : $\mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{O}=16$ and Avogadro's constant $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.0 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
question: The ratio of number of oxygen atoms $(\mathrm{O})$ in $16.0 \mathrm{~g}$ ozone $\left(\mathrm{O}_3\right), 28.0 \mathrm{~g}$ carbon monoxide $(\mathrm{CO})$ and $16.0$ oxygen $\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)$ is (Atomic mass : $\mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{O}=16$ and Avogadro's constant $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.0 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
solution:
$16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_3=\frac{16}{48}$ mole
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{16}{48} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules } \\
& =3 \times \frac{16}{48} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& =6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& 28.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}=\frac{28}{28} \text { mole }=1 \text { mole } \\
& =1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules } \\
& =1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& =6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& 16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_2=\frac{16}{16} \text { mole }=1 \mathrm{~mole}
\end{aligned}
$$
$=1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules
$=1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atoms
$=6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atoms
Therefore, the ratio is
$6.023 \times 10^{23}: 6.023 \times 10^{23}: 6.023 \times 10^{23}$
i.e. $1: 1: 1$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Assume carbon burns according to following equation :
2C(s)+O2( g)→2CO (s) 
when 12 g carbon is burnt in 48 g of oxygen, the volume of carbon monoxide produced is ______×10-1 L at STP [nearest integer]

[Given : Assume CO as ideal gas, Mass of C is 12 g mol-1, mass of O is 16 g mol-1 and molar volume of an idal gas at STP is 22.7 L mol-1 ]
question:
Assume carbon burns according to following equation :
2C(s)+O2( g)→2CO (s) 
when 12 g carbon is burnt in 48 g of oxygen, the volume of carbon monoxide produced is ______×10-1 L at STP [nearest integer]

[Given : Assume CO as ideal gas, Mass of C is 12 g mol-1, mass of O is 16 g mol-1 and molar volume of an idal gas at STP is 22.7 L mol-1 ]
solution:
Combustion of carbon under less supply of air,
2C+O2→2CO    ....(X)

1212 4832                (Given moles of reactants)
Thus, carbon is the limiting reagent as for 1.5 mol of Oxygen, 3 moles of carbon is required.

Thus, from equation(X) we get,

Mole of CO=1 mole

From arrhenius law,
VCOatSTP=22.7Lit

=227×10-1Lit

question:
Complete combustion of 3 g of ethane gives x×1022molecules of water. The value of x is ______ (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
Use :NA=6.023×1023; Atomic masses in u: C: 12.0 ; O: 16.0 ; H: 1.0
solution:
C2H6+ 72 O2→2CO2 + 3H2O

MWC2H6=30g

30g of C2H6  gives rise to 3 moles of H2O

3g of C2H6 gives rise to 0.3 moles of H2O

No. of molecules =0.3×6.023×1023
=18.069×1022

subject: Chemistry
q:
An aqueous KCl solution of density 1.20 g mL-1 has a molality of 3.30 mol kg-1. The molarity of the solution in molL-1 is____.(The Nearest integer)
[Molar mass of KCl=74.5 g]
question:
An aqueous KCl solution of density 1.20 g mL-1 has a molality of 3.30 mol kg-1. The molarity of the solution in molL-1 is____.(The Nearest integer)
[Molar mass of KCl=74.5 g]
solution:
Molality m=M×10001000×d-M×Msolute 

3.3=M×10001000×1.20-M×74.5

3960-245.85M=1000M

1245.85M=3960

M=39601245.85=3.17≈3M

subject: Chemistry
q: Dissolving 120 g of a compound of (mol. wt. 60) in 1000 g of water gave a solution of density 1.12 g/mL. The molarity of the solution is :
question: Dissolving 120 g of a compound of (mol. wt. 60) in 1000 g of water gave a solution of density 1.12 g/mL. The molarity of the solution is :
solution:
Mass of compound  =120g ;   moles= 120g 60g/mole =2

Mass of solution  = 1 0 0 0 + 1 2 0 = 1 1 2 0 g

Volume of solution  mass d = 1120g 1.12g/mL =1000mL

Molarity  = 2  moles 1 0 0 0  mL × 1 0 0 0 mL

              = 2 M (no of moles/Litre)

subject: Chemistry
q: A solution is prepared by adding <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mo>'</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>''</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> mole of water. Mass percent of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>''</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>''</mo></math>in solution is
question: A solution is prepared by adding <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mo>'</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>''</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> mole of water. Mass percent of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>''</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>''</mo></math>in solution is
solution:
To calculate the mass percent of ''X'' in the solution, we need to determine the mass of ''X'' and the total mass of the solution.

Given:

:Solute (X) = 2 g

Solvent (H2O) = 1 mole = 18 g

Total mass = 2 + 18 = 20 g

 Mass percent of X=22+18×100=10

subject: Chemistry
q: <math><mn>1</mn></math> gram of a carbonate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> on treatment with excess <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> produces <math><mn>0.01186</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> . The molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> in  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
question: <math><mn>1</mn></math> gram of a carbonate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> on treatment with excess <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> produces <math><mn>0.01186</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> . The molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> in  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
solution:
M2CO3+2HCl→2MCl+H2O+CO2

From reaction,

Moles of M2CO3=Moles of CO2

Weight of M2CO3Molar Mass of M2CO3 =Weight of CO2Molar mass of CO2

1Molar Mass of M2CO3 =0.01186 ×4444

 1Molar Mass of M2CO3=0.01186

Molar Mass  =10.01186=84.3 g/mol

subject: Chemistry
q: The hydrocarbon $(X)$ with molar mass $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $90 \%$ carbon has $\ldots\ldots$ degree of unsaturation.
question: The hydrocarbon $(X)$ with molar mass $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $90 \%$ carbon has $\ldots\ldots$ degree of unsaturation.
solution: $\therefore$ Empirical formula is $\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_4$ Molecular mass $=80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Molecular formula $=\left(\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_4\right)_{\mathrm{n}}$ $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\mathrm{EF} \text { mass }}=\frac{80}{40}=2$ $\therefore$ Molecular formula is $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_8$ Degree of unsaturation $=\frac{2 \times 6+2-8}{2}=3$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The reaction that occurs in a breath analyser, a device used to determine the alcohol level in a person's blood stream is

2 K2Cr2O7+8H2SO4+3C2H6O→2Cr2SO43+3C2H4O2+2 K2SO4+11H2O

If the rate of appearance of Cr2SO43 is 2.67 mol min-1 at a particular time, the rate of disappearance of C2H6O at the same time is mol min-1. (Nearest integer)
question:
The reaction that occurs in a breath analyser, a device used to determine the alcohol level in a person's blood stream is

2 K2Cr2O7+8H2SO4+3C2H6O→2Cr2SO43+3C2H4O2+2 K2SO4+11H2O

If the rate of appearance of Cr2SO43 is 2.67 mol min-1 at a particular time, the rate of disappearance of C2H6O at the same time is mol min-1. (Nearest integer)
solution:
13-dC2H6Odt=12dCr2SO43dt

13 (Rate of disappearance of C2H6O=12 (Rate of disappearance of Cr2SO43

Rate of disappearance of C2H6O=2.67 mol/min×32=4.005 mol/min

subject: Chemistry
q:
N2g+3H2g⇌2NH3g20 g       5 g

Consider the above reaction. If $20$g of dinitrogen reacts with $5$g of dihydrogen, then the limiting reagent of the reaction and number of moles of NH3 formed respectively are
question:
N2g+3H2g⇌2NH3g20 g       5 g

Consider the above reaction. If $20$g of dinitrogen reacts with $5$g of dihydrogen, then the limiting reagent of the reaction and number of moles of NH3 formed respectively are
solution:
N2g+3H2g⇌2NH3g20 g       5 g                         Given mass2028         52                          Number of moles

To find the limiting reagent we can divide given number of moles with Stoichiometric coefficients, whichever will be the lower will be limiting reagent.

N2→20281=2028  H2→523=56

∴ N2 is the Limiting Reagent.

According to the reaction one mole of nitrogen gas gives two moles of ammonia.

∴ nNH3=2×nN2=2×2028

Number of moles of ammonia formed will be =1.42

subject: Chemistry
q: The moles of methane required to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>81</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water after complete combustion is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></math>. [nearest integer]
question: The moles of methane required to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>81</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water after complete combustion is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></math>. [nearest integer]
solution:
CH4+2O2⟶CO2+2H2O

POAC on H atom

nCH4×4=nH2O×2

nCH4=8118×2×14=8136

nCH4=2.25

=225×10-2

Hence, the number of moles of methane is 225×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>86</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of aniline completely reacts to form acetanilide. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the product is lost during purification. Amount of acetanilide obtained after purification (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>) is ___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>86</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of aniline completely reacts to form acetanilide. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the product is lost during purification. Amount of acetanilide obtained after purification (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>) is ___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Given 1.86 g

⇒1 mol C6H5NH2 give 1 mol 

∴ moles of C6H5NH2= moles of 

⇒1.8693=Wacetanilide 135

⇒Wacelanilide =1.86×13593 g=2.70 g

But efficiency of reaction is 90% only

∴ Mass of acetanilide produced=2.70×90100 g

=2.43 g

=243×10-2 g

⇒x=243

subject: Chemistry
q: 20 mL of 2 M NaOH solution is added to 400 mL of 0.5 M NaOH solution. The final concentration of the solution is _______ $\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$. (Nearest integer)
question: 20 mL of 2 M NaOH solution is added to 400 mL of 0.5 M NaOH solution. The final concentration of the solution is _______ $\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$. (Nearest integer)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & M_F=\frac{M_1 V_1+M_2 V_2}{V_1+V_2} \\ & =\frac{2 \times 20+0.5 \times 400}{420}=0.571 \mathrm{M} \\ & =57.1 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M} \\ & =57\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Concentrated nitric acid is labelled as $75 \%$ by mass. The volume in mL of the solution which contains 30 g of nitric acid is 
____.
Given : Density of nitric acid solution is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$.
question:
Concentrated nitric acid is labelled as $75 \%$ by mass. The volume in mL of the solution which contains 30 g of nitric acid is 
____.
Given : Density of nitric acid solution is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$.
solution:
$75 \%$ by mass means :
$\because \quad 75 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{HNO}_3$ in 100 g solution.
$\therefore \quad 30 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{HNO}_3$ in $\frac{100}{75} \times 30 \mathrm{~g}$ solution.
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{m}_{\text {solution }}=\frac{100 \times 30}{75} \mathrm{~g} \\
\because \quad & \mathrm{~V}_{\text {sol }}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\text {sol }}}{\mathrm{d}_{\text {sol }}}=\frac{100 \times 30}{75 \times 1.25}=32 \mathrm{~mL}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
In the following reaction:

A+2B+3C⇌AB2C3
6.0 g of A, 6.0×1023  atoms of B and 0.036 mol of C reacted and formed 4.8 g of compound AB2C3. If the atomic mass of A  and C are 60 and 80 amu, respectively. What is the atomic mass of B in amu? (Avogadro number=6×1023)
question:
In the following reaction:

A+2B+3C⇌AB2C3
6.0 g of A, 6.0×1023  atoms of B and 0.036 mol of C reacted and formed 4.8 g of compound AB2C3. If the atomic mass of A  and C are 60 and 80 amu, respectively. What is the atomic mass of B in amu? (Avogadro number=6×1023)
solution:
A + 2 B + 3 C → AB 2 C 3 6 g 6 × 1 0 2 3 atoms 0.036 moles Moles 6 6 0 6 × 1 0 2 3 6 × 1 0 2 3 0.1 mole 1.0 mole 0.036 moles

0.11 ,1.02 ,0.0363; the least value is 0.012, i.e., C is the limiting reagent
0.036 moles of C provides 0.012 moles of AB2C3.

0.012 moles of AB2C3 weighs 4.8 g
1 mole of AB2C3 weighs 4.80.012=400 g mol-1
400=60+2B+3×80
B=50 amu

subject: Chemistry
q:
When 81.0 g of aluminium is allowed to react with 128.0 g of oxygen gas, the mass of aluminium oxide produced in grams is_______ - (Nearest integer)
Given :
Molar mass of Al is $27.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Molar mass of O is $16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
question:
When 81.0 g of aluminium is allowed to react with 128.0 g of oxygen gas, the mass of aluminium oxide produced in grams is_______ - (Nearest integer)
Given :
Molar mass of Al is $27.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Molar mass of O is $16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 4 \mathrm{Al}+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \\ & \frac{81}{27}=3 \text { mole } \frac{128}{32}=4 \mathrm{~mole}\end{aligned}$


Limiting reagent
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \text { mole of } \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \text { formed }=\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \text { mole } \\
& \therefore \text { wt. of } \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \text { formed }=\frac{3}{2} \times 102 \\
& \quad=153 \mathrm{gm}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Molality ( \(\mathrm{m}\) ) of \(3 \mathrm{M}\) aqueous solution of \(\mathrm{NaCl}\) is :
(Given : Density of solution \(=1.25 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\), Molar mass in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}: \mathrm{Na}-23, \mathrm{Cl}-35.5\))
question:
Molality ( \(\mathrm{m}\) ) of \(3 \mathrm{M}\) aqueous solution of \(\mathrm{NaCl}\) is :
(Given : Density of solution \(=1.25 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\), Molar mass in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}: \mathrm{Na}-23, \mathrm{Cl}-35.5\))
solution:
3 moles are present in 1 litre solution
$\text {molality }=\frac{3 \times 1000}{1.25 \times 1000-[3 \times 58.5]}=2.79 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Compound A contains 8.7% Hydrogen, 74% Carbon and 17.3% Nitrogen. The molecular formula of the compound is, Given : Atomic masses of C,H and N are 12,1 and 14 amu respectively.

The molar mass of the compound A is 162 g mol-1.
question:
Compound A contains 8.7% Hydrogen, 74% Carbon and 17.3% Nitrogen. The molecular formula of the compound is, Given : Atomic masses of C,H and N are 12,1 and 14 amu respectively.

The molar mass of the compound A is 162 g mol-1.
solution:
C 74% 7412=6.16 6.161.23=5
N 17.3% 17.314=1.23 1.231.23=1
H 8.7% 8.71=8.7 8.71.23=7

Emperical formula =C5NH7

Emperical weight =81

Multiplying factor =16281=2

Molecular formula=Empirical formulan

Molecular formula=C5NH72

Molecular formula =C10N2H14

subject: Chemistry
q: Density of 3 M NaCl solution is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. The molality of the solution is :
question: Density of 3 M NaCl solution is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. The molality of the solution is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 3 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaCl}, \mathrm{d}_{\text {sol }}=1.25 \mathrm{gm} / \mathrm{mol} \\ & \begin{aligned} \text { Molality } & =\frac{\mathrm{M} \times 1000}{1000 \mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{M} \times \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{W}}} \\ & =\frac{3000}{1250-175.5}=2.79\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
